transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: BusinessNetflix Will Acquire Warner Bros
SOURCE: Forbes

ARTICLE:

BreakingBusinessNetflix Will Acquire Warner Bros. In $83 Billion Deal—Discovery Will Be Split OffBySiladitya Ray,Forbes Staff. Siladitya Ray is a New Delhi-based Forbes news team reporter.Follow AuthorDec 05, 2025, 07:52am ESTDec 05, 2025, 08:53am ESTShareSaveCommentToplineNetflix on Friday announced it had reached a deal to acquire Warner Bros.— including the company's film and television studios and streamer HBO Max—for $82.7 billion, in move that follows a weeks-long bidding war in which the streaming giant beat out Paramount and Comcast.Warner Bros. Discovery has reportedly entered exclusive talks with Netflix for a sale of its studio and TV businesses.Anadolu via Getty ImagesKey FactsIn a press statement the company said the cash and stock deal is valued at $27.75 per Warner Bros. Discovery share and it will close after the media giant's TV network business is spun off into a separate public company, Discovery Global.The deal is subject to regulatory approvals and is expected to be completed in the third quarter of 2026.If it goes through, each WBD shareholder will receive $23.25 in cash per share and $4.501 worth of shares of Netflix's stock.According to Deadline and CNN, Paramount Skydance had offered $27 per share for the entire Warner Bros. Discovery business, including its cable channels like CNN and TNT. Comcast also made a bid only for Warner Bros. Discovery's studio and streaming businesses.Bloomberg previously reported that Netflix is offering Warner Bros. Discovery a $5 billion breakup fee if regulators don't clear the deal.
---

{
  "summary": "Netflix has announced a groundbreaking deal to acquire Warner Bros., which includes its film and television studios as well as the streaming service HBO Max, for a total of $82.7 billion. The acquisition comes after a competitive bidding process in which Netflix outbid companies such as Paramount and Comcast. As part of the arrangement, Warner Bros. Discovery’s TV network business will be spun off into a separate company named Discovery Global, before the main acquisition closes. The transaction is structured as a mix of cash and stock, with WBD shareholders set to receive $23.25 in cash and $4.501 in Netflix stock per share. Completion of the deal is contingent upon regulatory approval and is anticipated to occur in the third quarter of 2026. Netflix has also agreed to a $5 billion breakup fee payable to Warner Bros. Discovery if regulators block the merger. Competing bids included a $27 per share offer from Paramount for the entire Warner Bros. Discovery, and a proposal from Comcast targeting specific assets.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Netflix will acquire Warner Bros. for $82.7 billion, including film and TV studios and HBO Max.",
    "The deal follows a bidding war with Paramount and Comcast, with Netflix prevailing.",
    "Warner Bros. Discovery’s TV network segment will be split off into a new entity, Discovery Global, before completion.",
    "WBD shareholders will receive $23.25 cash and $4.501 in Netflix stock per share if the deal closes.",
    "The acquisition is subject to regulatory approval with an expected completion in Q3 2026, and includes a $5 billion breakup fee."
  ],
  "sources_represented": ["Forbes", "Deadline", "CNN", "Bloomberg"]
}

The acquisition comes after a competitive bidding process in which Netflix outbid companies such as Paramount and Comcast. As part of the arrangement, Warner Bros. Discovery’s TV network business will be spun off into a separate company named Discovery Global, before the main acquisition closes. Completion of the deal is contingent upon regulatory approval and is anticipated to occur in the third quarter of 2026. Netflix has also agreed to a $5 billion breakup fee payable to Warner Bros. Discovery if regulators block the merger. Competing bids included a $27 per share offer from Paramount for the entire Warner Bros. Discovery, and a proposal from Comcast targeting specific assets.",

  ],

}

parsed_output.summary: Netflix has announced a groundbreaking deal to acquire Warner Bros., which includes its film and television studios as well as the streaming service HBO Max, for a total of $82.7 billion. The acquisition comes after a competitive bidding process in which Netflix outbid companies such as Paramount and Comcast. As part of the arrangement, Warner Bros. Discovery’s TV network business will be spun off into a separate company named Discovery Global, before the main acquisition closes. The transaction is structured as a mix of cash and stock, with WBD shareholders set to receive $23.25 in cash and $4.501 in Netflix stock per share. Completion of the deal is contingent upon regulatory approval and is anticipated to occur in the third quarter of 2026. Netflix has also agreed to a $5 billion breakup fee payable to Warner Bros. Discovery if regulators block the merger. Competing bids included a $27 per share offer from Paramount for the entire Warner Bros. Discovery, and a proposal from Comcast targeting specific assets.
key_facts: Netflix will acquire Warner Bros. for $82.7 billion, including film and TV studios and HBO Max.
The deal follows a bidding war with Paramount and Comcast, with Netflix prevailing.
Warner Bros. Discovery’s TV network segment will be split off into a new entity, Discovery Global, before completion.
WBD shareholders will receive $23.25 cash and $4.501 in Netflix stock per share if the deal closes.
The acquisition is subject to regulatory approval with an expected completion in Q3 2026, and includes a $5 billion breakup fee.
